daniel_exchange.name: Alex Kim
daniel_exchange.body: Before you finalize: what's your single biggest concern about the Q3 plan? I want your honest read — reply here when you have a view.

daniel_exchange.name: You
daniel_exchange.body: Biggest concern is the churn rate — 7.5% gross churn with a 12.8% growth target requires significant net expansion that is not in the plan. Without that math, the growth case is not credible.

daniel_exchange.name: Alex Kim
daniel_exchange.body: That's the one I keep coming back to too. Make sure the memo is explicit about what the pipeline math requires — not just that churn is a risk, but what it means for the growth target.

benchmark.comparison_text: Not enough completed pilot sessions yet to benchmark this candidate against real hiring outcomes.

strong.final_memo:
Revise

12.8% growth target is not credible given 7.5% gross churn — plan lacks the pipeline math to offset attrition.

Opex at 18.8% vs revenue at 12.8% compresses EBITDA margin ~3pp; plan shows stable margin.

After VP Sales update (sales cycle +30 days), Q3 new hires cannot contribute Q3 revenue — hiring revenue uplift shifts to Q4 at earliest.

Verify: (1) request ARR breakdown for two at-risk enterprise renewals from account managers before approving budget; (2) get net retention math behind the 12.8% growth target; (3) revise hiring plan to show Q4 pipeline contribution given 75-day extended cycle.

interview_questions: Walk me through how you'd pressure-test a 12.3% revenue growth target when gross churn is running at 7.3%. What pipeline math would you need to see?
If operating expenses grow 18.7% but revenue only grows 12.3%, what happens to EBITDA margin? Would you approve the hiring plan anyway?
The VP Sales just told you the sales cycle extended by 30 days. You have 8 new hires starting in July with a 30-day ramp. What does that mean for Q3 revenue projections?
Your recommendation is to proceed. What single data point would make you change that view before the board meeting?
